Q1: I kept things pretty structured, honestly. I'd block out about two to three hours on certain days of the week specifically for coursework. I'm a visual learner, so I'd organize my notes with color-coding and diagrams. Having a routine helped me stay on track without feeling overwhelmed, even though I was juggling a lot of online courses at once.
Q2: I tried to stick to regular times, usually Tuesday and Thursday evenings and some time on Sundays. But I'd be lying if I said it never varied—when deadlines piled up, I'd shift things around. The core routine stayed the same though; I just added extra sessions when needed rather than completely abandoning my schedule.
Q3: Usually it was checking if new materials were posted or if there were announcements about assignments. I'd also log in when I knew a deadline was coming up to double-check submission requirements. Sometimes I'd get an email notification and that would prompt me to go in and see what was happening.
Q4: Yes, after the first few weeks it became automatic. Every Tuesday evening after dinner, I'd just open my laptop and go to the LMS without even thinking about it. It became like brushing my teeth—just part of my day. Having that consistency made it less of a mental burden to get started.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. I also found the assignment rubrics helpful because they gave me a clear picture of what was expected. Anything visual or clearly structured worked well for my learning style.
Q6: I mostly ignored the discussion forums. I posted maybe three times the whole semester because they felt more like busywork than genuine learning opportunities. I also didn't engage much with supplementary readings unless they were directly tied to assignments. Time was limited with fifteen courses, so I had to prioritize.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand a concept. Visual explanations, practice problems with solutions, things like that. If something felt vague or disconnected from the main content, I'd skip it without much guilt.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different—like they were nice-to-haves rather than essentials. With required work, I'd push through even if I wasn't in the mood. Optional stuff? I'd only do it if I had extra energy, which honestly wasn't often. The pressure just wasn't there.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my tablet without being connected. I also made my own visual summaries and flashcards that I'd review away from the computer. A lot of my actual learning happened with printed materials spread out on my desk.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd be reviewing my handwritten notes or working through problems on paper. None of that would show up anywhere in the system. I'd also discuss concepts with a friend over coffee sometimes, which was genuinely helpful but completely invisible to any tracking.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd watch a video once to get the gist, download the slides, and then work through them multiple times offline. The LMS would only show that single video view, but I might have spent hours with that material afterward. It was more efficient for me to study without constant internet distractions.
Q12: Around midterms, things got harder. I was managing fifteen online courses simultaneously, and even with good organization, the workload became intense. I remember feeling surprised by how quickly everything piled up. My attendance dipped during that period because I had to make tough choices about where to focus.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or had competing deadlines, I'd do the minimum on the LMS—just enough to stay on track. I'd skim materials instead of deeply engaging. The stress was high during certain weeks, and I had to be strategic about my energy. Some courses got more attention than others.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through readings or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I was technically 'active' but not really absorbing anything. It usually happened when I was tired but trying to stick to my routine anyway. Going through the motions isn't the same as learning.
Q15: I'd sometimes postpone discussion posts because they felt low-priority compared to assignments. Even knowing participation mattered somewhat, I'd push it off until the last minute or skip it entirely. The return on investment just didn't seem worth it when I had assignments due in other courses.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my compass. I'd structure my week around what was due and when. The days before a deadline, my LMS activity would spike as I'd be checking requirements, uploading work, maybe reviewing materials one more time. Without deadlines, I might have drifted more.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, though it sometimes came later than I'd hoped. When I got good feedback, it motivated me to maintain my approach. Critical feedback made me pay closer attention to similar assignments afterward. The CBT session I had also helped me process feedback without getting too stressed about it.
Q18: The course structure gave me a clear roadmap, which I appreciated. Weekly modules with predictable patterns made it easy to plan. I could look ahead and know what was coming, which suited my routine-oriented approach. Without that structure, I would have struggled more to stay organized.
Q19: Clear visual organization of materials made engagement easier—when everything was logically laid out, I could navigate efficiently. What made it harder was when resources were buried in subfolders or when instructions were text-heavy without visual aids. Consistency in design across modules really helped me.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information, trying to understand what's expected, or feeling lost. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. They could be clicking around confused, or maybe they're perfectionists who keep reviewing but struggle to actually submit. It's not always straightforward.
Q21: That actually reflects my experience somewhat. My exam score wasn't great, but I passed and completed most assignments. A lot of my studying happened offline with downloaded materials and my own notes. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with physical materials away from the screen.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, completion rates. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and whether someone actually understood the material. They can't capture a student reviewing printed notes at a coffee shop or discussing concepts with peers.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material, making connections, and caring about understanding it—not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious and invested, not just going through motions. True engagement is mental and emotional involvement, which doesn't always correlate with clicks and logins.
Q24: My offline study habits, the visual summaries I created, conversations with peers, and the mental processing I did while commuting or before sleep—none of that shows up. Also, the CBT session helped my resilience, but the data wouldn't show how that internal work affected my ability to stay consistent despite stress.
